skill.name: edit-article
skill.description: Edit and improve articles by restructuring sections, improving clarity, and tightening prose. Use when user wants to edit, revise, or improve an article draft.
skill.summary: A structured writing skill that helps you revise article drafts through section-based editing and clarity improvements.
skill.author: mattpocock

security_audit.summary: The skill contains only a natural language writing guide with no executable code. The static analyzer flagged a 'weak cryptographic algorithm' pattern at line 3, but this is a false positive: line 3 is a YAML frontmatter description field containing a sentence about editing articles, with no cryptographic content. The file poses no security risk.

low_findings.title: False positive: Weak cryptographic algorithm pattern (dismissed)
low_findings.description: The static analyzer reported a 'Weak cryptographic algorithm' pattern at SKILL.md:3. Manual review confirms line 3 is a YAML frontmatter description field ('description: Edit and improve articles by restructuring sections, improving clarity, and tightening prose...'). No cryptographic code, algorithms, or hash functions exist anywhere in the file. The file contains only natural language instructions for editing prose. This finding is dismissed as a false positive.

content.user_title: Edit and Improve Article Drafts with Structured Revision
content.value_statement: Editing an article draft can feel overwhelming when sections, flow, and clarity all need attention. This skill provides a clear, section-by-section revision process that tightens prose and improves coherence.
actual_capabilities: Divides an article into sections based on its existing headings.
Reorders sections to respect information dependencies as a directed acyclic graph.
Rewrites each section to improve clarity, coherence, and flow.
Enforces a maximum of 240 characters per paragraph for readable prose.
Confirms section structure with the user before making detailed edits.
limitations: Requires an article draft with heading-based structure to divide into sections.
Does not generate new content from scratch; it edits existing drafts.
Relies on the user to confirm section divisions before rewriting begins.
Character limit guidance applies only to paragraphs, not headings or lists.

use_cases.title: Revise a technical blog post draft
use_cases.description: A developer has a rough blog post draft and wants it restructured for clarity and tighter prose before publishing.

use_cases.title: Polish a tutorial or how-to article
use_cases.description: A tutorial author needs each section rewritten to improve flow and ensure information builds logically from one section to the next.

use_cases.title: Edit newsletter or essay drafts
use_cases.description: A writer wants their essay or newsletter draft divided into clear sections and refined for readability and coherence.

prompt_templates.title: Basic article revision
prompt_templates.prompt: Please edit and improve the following article draft. Divide it into sections, confirm the structure with me, then rewrite each section for clarity and flow.
prompt_templates.scenario: First-time use with a complete draft

prompt_templates.title: Restructure for information flow
prompt_templates.prompt: Here is my article draft. Divide it into sections based on headings, then reorder the sections so that information dependencies are respected. Confirm the new order with me before rewriting.
prompt_templates.scenario: Reordering a draft with tangled topics

prompt_templates.title: Tighten prose in each section
prompt_templates.prompt: Divide this article into sections. For each section, rewrite the prose to improve clarity, coherence, and flow. Keep paragraphs at a maximum of 240 characters.
prompt_templates.scenario: Improving readability of an existing draft

prompt_templates.title: Full editing workflow
prompt_templates.prompt: Act as my article editor. Step 1: Divide the draft into sections based on headings and confirm with me. Step 2: For each section, rewrite for clarity, coherence, and flow with a maximum of 240 characters per paragraph.

output_examples.input: An article draft about JavaScript closures with sections in a non-optimal order.
output: Section division: 'What is a closure?' / 'Creating closures' / 'Closures in practice' / 'Common pitfalls'
Section order adjusted so 'What is a closure?' comes first as a dependency for all other sections.
Each section rewritten with shorter paragraphs and clearer explanations.
All paragraphs kept under 240 characters for readability.

output_examples.input: A draft paragraph that is too long and hard to follow.
output: Original paragraph split into two shorter paragraphs.
Main idea placed in the first sentence of each paragraph.
Supporting details moved to follow-up sentences.
Character count verified under 240 per paragraph.

best_practices: Always confirm the section structure with the user before rewriting individual sections.
Think about information dependencies between sections before reordering them.
Keep paragraphs to a maximum of 240 characters to maintain readability.
anti_patterns: Do not rewrite sections before confirming the section division and order with the user.
Do not ignore information dependencies when reordering sections.
Do not produce paragraphs longer than 240 characters in the final output.

faq.question: What does this skill do?
faq.answer: It edits and improves article drafts by dividing them into sections, respecting information dependencies, and rewriting for clarity and flow.

faq.question: Does it write new articles from scratch?
faq.answer: No. It edits existing drafts. You need to provide an article draft with headings for it to work with.

faq.question: How does it decide section order?
faq.answer: It treats information as a directed acyclic graph and reorders sections so that dependent information comes after the information it depends on.

faq.question: Is there a paragraph length limit?
faq.answer: Yes. Each paragraph is rewritten to a maximum of 240 characters to keep prose tight and readable.

faq.question: Does it confirm changes with the user?
faq.answer: Yes. It asks the user to confirm the section structure before rewriting individual sections.

faq.question: Which AI tools does it support?
faq.answer: It works with Claude, Codex, and Claude Code, since it is a pure prompt-based skill with no code dependencies.
